title: Unsupervised Learning: Finding Patterns with No Answer Key
concept: Unsupervised learning explores data that has no labels, discovering its own groupings and patterns instead of checking guesses against a correct answer.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In supervised learning, what does every training example come with?
exercises.explain: Right! Supervised learning always trains on examples paired with the correct answer.

cards.title: Sorting Socks With No Instructions
cards.body: Dump a giant pile of mismatched socks on the floor and start sorting — nobody told you the categories. You might end up grouping by color, or by size, or by pattern. There was no answer key, yet you still found useful groups.

cards.title: What is unsupervised learning?
cards.body: Unsupervised learning works with data that has no labels at all. Instead of checking guesses against correct answers, the model looks for structure — groups, clusters, or patterns — hiding inside the data on its own.

cards.title: No teacher, no answer key
cards.body: If supervised learning is a teacher grading homework, unsupervised learning is being handed a messy box of photos with zero captions and told, "organize these however makes sense." There's no right answer to check against — just patterns to discover.

cards.title: Finding customer groups nobody named
cards.body: A store's data has no label saying "bargain hunter" or "big spender." An unsupervised model can still look at purchase histories and discover that customers naturally split into a few distinct groups — patterns a human might never have thought to look for.

cards.title: No labels doesn't mean no learning
cards.body: It's tempting to think that without correct answers, a model can't learn anything useful. In reality, unsupervised learning often uncovers patterns humans missed — grouping similar items together even though nobody ever defined the categories in advance.

cards.title: With labels vs. without labels
cards.body: The core difference from supervised learning:

left.text:
Has correct answers
Checks guess vs. label

right.text:
No correct answers
Groups data by similarity

exercises.prompt: What is the key trait of the data used in unsupervised learning?

options.text: It has no labels or correct answers
It must be sorted by a human first

exercises.explain: Right! Unsupervised learning works with completely unlabeled data.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An unsupervised model checks its output against a correct answer key, just like supervised learning does.
exercises.explain: False! There is no answer key in unsupervised learning — it finds patterns on its own.

exercises.sentence: Since there is no answer key, unsupervised learning looks for ___ hiding inside the data.
exercises.explain: Without labels to check against, unsupervised learning searches for patterns and groupings instead.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is an example of unsupervised learning?

options.text: A program that groups thousands of songs into clusters of similar-sounding tracks, with no pre-made genre labels
A program trained on photos each labeled "sunny" or "rainy"
A robot that earns points for successfully picking up an object

exercises.explain: Correct! Grouping songs with no genre labels given is exactly what unsupervised learning does.

exercises.prompt: A music app looks at millions of listening histories with no genre tags attached and notices that certain groups of listeners always play similar songs together, so it creates listener groups on its own. Why is this unsupervised learning, not supervised?

options.text: Because there were no correct labels to check the groupings against — the model discovered the groups itself
Because the app rewarded the model with points for each correct genre guess
Because supervised learning cannot work with music data
Because the model asked a human to grade every grouping

exercises.explain: Exactly! With no labels to check against, the app had to discover the listener groups purely from patterns in the data — that's unsupervised learning.
